worth_it_explanation: Ad quality scanning is the line of defense between the open auction and a publisher's audience, so it decides whether a malicious or policy-breaking creative reaches the page. A scan that is switched off, or one that flags creatives without anyone acting on the flags, lets malware, forced redirects, and policy violations through to readers, which costs reader trust and can draw the page into a security incident. The check is a review of the ad quality controls a publisher already has in PubMatic, so it costs reading time rather than rebuild time, and the finding points to a concrete control the publisher can turn on or a flagged-creative workflow they can fix.
intro: Ad quality scanning is the screening that PubMatic runs on creatives before they reach a publisher's page, catching malware, forced redirects, and policy violations in the bid stream rather than after a reader has been harmed. PubMatic's Real-Time Ad Scanning (RTAS) infrastructure works with security partner Confiant to inspect each creative for malicious code and technical problems. This subcategory is about whether that scanning is enabled for the publisher's inventory and whether the publisher acts on what the scan flags. These checks confirm that ad quality protection is an active control rather than a setting left at its default, and that flagged creatives are reviewed and blocked instead of left running.

audit_checks.title: Creative scanning for malware and policy violations is not enabled
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Creative scanning is what keeps malware, forced redirects, and undeclared audio off a publisher's page before a reader is exposed. When scanning is off or scoped to only part of the inventory, malicious and policy-breaking creatives can serve to real readers, which risks a security incident on the page and the loss of reader trust, so the gap is closer to catastrophic than to routine hygiene.

educate.base:
PubMatic screens creatives before they reach a publisher's page through its Real-Time Ad Scanning (RTAS) infrastructure, which works with security partner Confiant to inspect each creative in the bid stream. The scan looks for malicious code that forces or tricks a reader into an unwanted action, for auto-redirects that pull the reader off the page, and for technical problems such as heavy creatives, video errors, and ads with undeclared audio. The point of scanning the creative before it serves is that a malicious ad never reaches a real reader rather than being cleaned up after the harm is done.

Malvertising is the reason the scan matters. A creative laced with hostile code can execute on the reader's device, attempt to install software, or hijack the page to a destination the reader never chose. When that creative serves on a publisher's inventory, the reader experiences the attack on the publisher's page, so the cost lands on the publisher's reputation regardless of which buyer supplied the ad. Scanning every creative, rather than a sample, is what closes the path for those creatives to reach the page.

The audit question is whether scanning is enabled across the publisher's inventory or whether it is off, scoped to part of the inventory, or assumed to be on without confirmation. Because the protection runs at the platform level, a publisher may believe it is covered when a portion of their inventory, a specific format, or a specific deal path is not in scope. Confirming coverage is the difference between protection that is documented and protection that is assumed.

A few signs point to creative scanning that is not fully enabled:
  • Ad quality scanning is off, or the publisher cannot confirm whether it is on, for some or all of their inventory
  • A format such as video or CTV, or a specific deal path, runs without confirmation that scanning covers it
  • The publisher has no record of the ad quality settings PubMatic applies to their inventory
You may want to confirm that creative scanning is enabled across all of the publisher's inventory and formats. One approach is to review the ad quality settings PubMatic applies to the account with the platform team and record which inventory and formats the scan covers.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media site, a malicious creative that forces a redirect off an article page reaches a reader who came to read, so the harm registers as the publisher's fault even though a buyer supplied the ad. You may want to confirm that scanning covers every placement a reader can encounter, including in-article and below-content units, rather than only the high-traffic homepage inventory.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content site, a forced redirect or a malware prompt during a shopping session can break the path to a purchase and erode the trust the site depends on to convert. You may want to confirm that scanning covers the product and category page inventory, where readers are closest to a transaction, with the same coverage as the editorial inventory.

learn_more.source_title: SafeFrame: secure ad container that prevents malicious ad behaviors

investigate.intro: Review whether ad quality scanning is enabled for the publisher's inventory and which formats and deal paths it covers.

pubmatic_console.label: PubMatic console
steps: Open the PubMatic publisher console and locate the ad quality or creative settings for the account.
Record whether creative scanning is enabled and which inventory, formats, and deal paths it covers.
Note any inventory, format such as video or CTV, or deal path that runs without confirmed scanning coverage.
Ask the PubMatic platform team to confirm the ad quality settings applied to the account and record their answer.

creative_inspection.label: Creative inspection
steps: Load a sample of the publisher's pages across formats and watch for any creative that triggers a redirect, an unexpected download, or undeclared audio.
Use the browser developer tools network panel to check whether a served creative attempts a redirect to a destination the reader did not choose.
Record any creative behavior that a working scan would be expected to catch, along with the page and format where it appeared.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records whether creative scanning is enabled and which inventory, formats, and deal paths it covers. An account where scanning is confirmed across all inventory and formats passes. An account where scanning is off, where coverage cannot be confirmed, or where a format or deal path runs without scanning is a fail recorded with the inventory and formats left unprotected.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record whether creative scanning is enabled and which inventory and formats it covers above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}} runs inventory without confirmed creative scanning across all formats and deal paths. PubMatic's Real-Time Ad Scanning (RTAS) screens creatives for malware, forced redirects, and technical problems before they reach the page, so inventory left out of scope can serve a malicious or policy-breaking creative to a real reader. {{voice_recommend}} confirming with the PubMatic platform team that ad quality scanning is enabled across every part of {{site_name}}'s inventory, including video, CTV, and any separate deal paths, then recording which inventory and formats the scan covers. After the change, keep a record of the ad quality settings so the coverage can be re-verified rather than assumed.

audit_checks.title: Flagged creatives are left active rather than reviewed and blocked
audit_checks.impact_rationale: A scan that flags a problem creative still lets that creative serve until someone acts on the flag. When flagged creatives sit unreviewed, the detection does not translate into protection, so policy-breaking or low-quality ads keep reaching readers and the value of the scan is lost on the creatives it caught.

educate.base:
Detection and enforcement are two separate steps. PubMatic's ad quality scanning categorizes each creative and can block or allow it based on the publisher's settings, and it surfaces flagged creatives for review. The scan finding a problem does not, on its own, stop the creative from serving on inventory where the publisher's controls allow it. The protection is complete only when a flagged creative is reviewed and a decision is made to block it, restrict it, or let it through on purpose.

The gap shows up when flags accumulate without a workflow to act on them. A creative flagged for undeclared audio, for a policy category the publisher does not want, or for a technical fault keeps reaching readers if no one reviews the flag and applies a block. The publisher's own controls in PubMatic, such as advertiser and category blocks, are what turn a flag into a block, so an unused control leaves the flagged creative active.

The audit question is whether the publisher has a workflow for the creatives the scan flags. That means someone reviews the flagged creatives on a regular cadence, applies blocks where the creative breaks the publisher's standards, and keeps the advertiser and category block lists current so repeat offenders stay out. Without that loop, the scan generates findings that no one converts into action.

A few signs point to flagged creatives that are not acted on:
  • Flagged creatives accumulate in the console with no record of review or of a block decision
  • Advertiser and category block lists have not been updated despite repeat flags against the same buyers or categories
  • No one owns the cadence of reviewing flagged creatives, so the queue is checked rarely or not at all
You may want to confirm that flagged creatives are reviewed on a set cadence and that the publisher's block controls are used to act on them. One approach is to assign an owner for the flagged-creative queue and to update the advertiser and category block lists whenever a repeat offender appears.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media site, a flagged creative that keeps serving while it waits for review reaches readers across every article it appears on, so an unworked queue compounds the exposure quickly. You may want to set a regular cadence for reviewing flagged creatives and keep the category block list aligned with the editorial standards the publisher holds direct-sold buyers to.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content site, a flagged creative for a competing retailer or an off-brand category can sit beside the products the site sells if no one reviews and blocks it. You may want to keep the advertiser block list current with competitors and unwanted categories so flagged creatives in those groups are stopped rather than left active.

educate.learn_more_note: PubMatic's detailed configuration documentation for the flagged-creative review queue and the advertiser and category block controls is in the partner portal (login required).

investigate.intro: Review whether the publisher has a workflow for the creatives the scan flags, including who reviews them, how often, and whether block controls are kept current.

pubmatic_console.label: PubMatic console
steps: Open the PubMatic publisher console and locate the flagged or blocked creatives and the advertiser and category block lists.
Record whether flagged creatives have been reviewed and whether block decisions are on record, or whether the queue has accumulated unworked.
Check whether the advertiser and category block lists have been updated in response to repeat flags against the same buyers or categories.
Ask the publisher who owns the flagged-creative review and how often the queue is checked, and record the answer.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records whether flagged creatives are reviewed on a cadence, whether block decisions are applied, and whether the advertiser and category block lists are kept current. An account with an owned review cadence and block controls used to act on flags passes. An account where flagged creatives accumulate unreviewed and block lists are stale is a fail recorded with the unworked queue and the controls left unused.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record whether flagged creatives are reviewed and whether block controls are kept current above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}} lets flagged creatives stay active rather than reviewing and blocking them. PubMatic's scan categorizes and flags creatives, but a flag stops a creative only when someone reviews it and applies a block through the publisher's controls, so an unworked queue lets policy-breaking and low-quality ads keep reaching readers. {{voice_recommend}} assigning an owner for the flagged-creative queue, reviewing it on a set cadence, and keeping the advertiser and category block lists current so repeat offenders stay out. After the change, recheck the queue to confirm flagged creatives are being acted on rather than accumulating.
